skill.name: relight
skill.description: Relight a still image — change the lighting setup, color temperature, direction, or mood — on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes to Qwen Edit 2509's dedicated `relight` LoRA endpoint for purpose-built relighting, with fallback to identity-preserving edit endpoints (Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, FLUX Kontext Pro) when prose lighting language is enough. Use for product relighting (studio softbox → window light), portrait mood shift (overcast → golden hour), or color-grade change. Triggers on "relight", "relighting", "change the lighting", "make it golden hour", "studio lighting", "rim light", "blue hour", "soft window light", "change light direction", "color temperature", or any explicit ask to alter how a still is lit.
skill.summary: AI-powered image relighting via RunComfy CLI — change lighting direction, color temperature, and mood while preserving subject identity.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The skill is a legitimate image relighting tool that uses the RunComfy CLI. The 93 static findings are predominantly false positives: the 'external_commands' flags detect legitimate bash invocations of the `runcomfy` CLI (scoped to `Bash(runcomfy *)`), the 'network' flags identify documentation URLs and placeholder examples, and the 'filesystem' flag references the standard config path `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` which is explicitly documented as a security feature (token stored with mode 0600). The skill includes a comprehensive Security & Privacy section that documents its network allowlist, token storage, and bash scope. No prompt injection attempts, data exfiltration, or malicious patterns were found. Risk is low due to the documented network and bash requirements, both of which are appropriately scoped.

low_findings.title: Network access required for RunComfy API
low_findings.description: The skill requires network access to model-api.runcomfy.net and related domains to submit relighting jobs and download results. This is documented in the skill's Security & Privacy section with an explicit allowlist. No telemetry or data exfiltration is performed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network access is clearly documented as required for the CLI to function, with explicit endpoint allowlist. This is expected behavior for a cloud API client.

low_findings.title: Bash scope limited to runcomfy commands
low_findings.description: The skill declares allowed-tools as Bash(runcomfy *) which restricts shell execution to runcomfy CLI invocations only. This is a well-scoped permission boundary that prevents arbitrary command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and restricts bash usage to the runcomfy CLI. This is a security best practice.

low_findings.title: Token storage in user config directory
low_findings.description: The runcomfy login command writes the API token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. This is the standard XDG config path and the restrictive permission limits access to the file owner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Token storage is documented with explicit file path and permission mode. The 0600 permission is the recommended setting for credential files.

content.user_title: Relight images with AI-powered lighting control
content.value_statement: Changing the lighting of a photograph traditionally requires reshooting or advanced Photoshop skills. This skill routes to purpose-built AI relighting models that change light direction, color temperature, and mood while preserving the subject's identity and composition.
actual_capabilities: Change lighting direction in still images (left, right, top, rim, etc.)
Apply time-of-day presets (golden hour, blue hour, overcast, high-noon)
Preserve subject identity, pose, and framing during relighting
Route between four AI models based on the relighting task complexity
Process multiple images in batch with consistent lighting
limitations: Requires a RunComfy account and API token
Best results with clearly described lighting intent in prompts
Some models may drift from subject identity without explicit preservation prompts

use_cases.title: Product catalog relighting
use_cases.description: Transform product photos from studio lighting to lifestyle settings. Change a white-box catalog shot to warm window light for an e-commerce lifestyle campaign.

use_cases.title: Portrait mood enhancement
use_cases.description: Shift portrait lighting from flat overcast to dramatic golden hour. Add rim lights or soft window light to improve subject dimensionality.

use_cases.title: Reference-based lighting matching
use_cases.description: Apply the lighting style of a reference photo to another image. Use when you need consistent lighting across a series of shots taken in different conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic product relight
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight this product photo as golden-hour studio: warm 3200K key light from camera-left at 45 degrees, soft cool fill from right, no rim light, preserve product orientation and color identity.
prompt_templates.scenario: Product photography with controlled studio lighting

prompt_templates.title: Portrait mood shift
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight as soft window light from the left, late-afternoon warm color temperature. Add subtle shadow on the right side of the face. Preserve identity and skin tones.
prompt_templates.scenario: Portrait photography needing natural light conversion

prompt_templates.title: Reference-based lighting match
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the lighting (direction, color temperature, contrast) of image 2 to image 1. Preserve image 1's subject identity, pose, and framing.

prompt_templates.title: Landscape time-of-day swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight as blue hour: cool 6500K ambient, soft fill from sky, no harsh shadows. Preserve all scene elements and composition.
prompt_templates.scenario: Converting landscape photos between times of day

output_examples.input: Product photo: white background, flat studio lighting, a ceramic vase centered in frame
output_examples.output: Relit product photo: warm window light from camera-left, soft shadow on the right side of the vase, late-afternoon color temperature, white background preserved

output_examples.input: Portrait: outdoor overcast lighting, subject facing camera, neutral expression
output_examples.output: Relit portrait: golden hour rim light from behind, warm soft key from front-left, subject identity and pose preserved

best_practices: Lead with the lighting type, then quantify: specify light source, color temperature, direction, and intensity in that order
Always include explicit preservation instructions for subject identity, pose, and color fidelity
Choose the right model: use Qwen Edit Relight for dedicated lighting work, Nano Banana 2 Edit for composite edits
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for generating images from scratch — use the ai-image-generation skill instead
Avoid vague lighting descriptions like 'make it look better' — specify direction, temperature, and intensity
Do not skip the preservation prompt — without it, models may change the subject's identity or pose

faq.question: What is the difference between the four models?
faq.answer: Qwen Edit Relight is purpose-built for lighting changes with maximum fidelity. Nano Banana 2 Edit handles lighting as part of broader composite edits. GPT Image 2 Edit works well with reference images. FLUX Kontext Pro is best for surgical single-instruction tweaks.

faq.question: Can this skill relight video content?
faq.answer: No, this skill is image-only. For video relighting, RunComfy offers ComfyUI workflows based on IC-Light variants that are available through their workflow catalog.

faq.question: How is the API token stored?
faq.answer: The token is written to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600, which restricts access to the file owner. In CI or container environments, set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.

faq.question: What network endpoints does the CLI contact?
faq.answer: The CLI only contacts model-api.runcomfy.net and related runcomfy.net and runcomfy.com domains. There is no telemetry or analytics traffic.

faq.question: Can I batch-process multiple images with the same lighting?
faq.answer: Yes, use Nano Banana 2 Edit with the image_urls array parameter to apply the same lighting prompt across multiple images in a single request.

faq.question: What happens if the model changes the subject's identity?
faq.answer: Add explicit preservation language to your prompt: 'preserve subject identity, pose, and framing'. If the issue persists, try the Qwen Edit Relight model which has stronger identity preservation.
